posts.headline: Claude Tag — Claude becomes a persistent, async Slack teammate
posts.body_summary: Replaces the old 'Claude in Slack' app with an always-on agent that lives in channels as a shared team member with its own service account; admins scope separate Claude identities, memory accumulates per channel. Ambient mode monitors channels and acts unprompted, async over hours/days. Beta for Enterprise & Team. Anthropic says its Claude Code team now writes ~65% of product code with Claude.

posts.headline: "The third LLM UI/UX paradigm"

source.grounding: his framing (post gated; quoted from the brief)

posts.body_summary: A mental model for where agent surfaces are heading: website → local app → a self-contained, persistent, asynchronous entity with org-wide tools & memory, working alongside teams. Framed as the substantive lens behind the Claude Tag shift.

posts.headline: Self-Harness: agents that rewrite their own harness
posts.body_summary: Three-stage loop — Weakness Mining → Harness Proposal → Proposal Validation (accept only after regression testing). Tested on Terminal-Bench-2.0 across MiniMax M2.5, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, GLM-5; held-out pass rates rise 40.5→61.9%, 23.8→38.1%, 42.9→57.1%. Weights frozen; only prompts/tools/policies change.

posts.headline: Does it actually generalize? Reading the fine print
posts.body_summary: Companion deep-dive to the Self-Harness paper: Terminal-Bench ships deterministic verifiers (not LLM-judged), so the real question is overfitting. The held-out gate is only 21 tasks reused across rounds → selection-overfitting; both splits come from the same benchmark, so nothing proves off-Terminal-Bench generalization. Net: it works because the domain ships a hard verifier — build the oracle, not the loop.

posts.headline: GLM-5.2 tops open coding leaderboards — at ~1/6 the cost
posts.body_summary: SWE-bench Pro 62.1 (vs GPT-5.5 58.6); FrontierSWE 74.4% (GPT-5.5 72.6, Opus 4.8 75.1); Terminal-Bench 2.1 81.0 (first open weights >80); ~1/6 the cost ($5.80 vs ~$35 / 1M tok). 753B MoE, ~40B active, 1M context, MIT license.

posts.headline: Small open model + structured search holds its own at vuln-finding
posts.body_summary: AISLE's nano-analyzer hunts zero-days function-by-function via a three-stage LLM pipeline (context gen → vuln scan → skeptical triage), outputting structured Markdown/JSON. Tuned for C/C++ memory safety. Claim: matched Mythos on public zero-day CVEs using open models, air-gappable. A cost result, not a superlative.

posts.headline: ASR that's conditioned on the dialogue turn
posts.body_summary: Built on Turn objects + immutable transcripts; each Turn maps to a speaking turn with intelligent endpointing and audio-cue turn detection. Keyterms prompting feeds a live list of up to ~100 domain terms over the socket. ~8.1% WER — lowest among streaming providers.

posts.headline: eve: Vercel's filesystem-first agent framework
posts.body_summary: An agent is a directory: instructions + skills as markdown, tools as TypeScript (defineTool + Zod), evals as scored CI suites. eve picks up tools/skills at build time, like Next.js file routing. Core premise: 'agents have a shape' — a recurring structure formalized so teams stop rebuilding plumbing.

posts.headline: Batteries included — the skeleton every agent ships with
posts.body_summary: eve's production capabilities: durable execution (sessions checkpoint each step, resume across crashes), sandboxed compute, subagents (scoped tools + clean contexts), and OpenTelemetry-compatible tracing on every run.

posts.headline: Nemotron 3 Ultra — efficient reasoning for long-running agents
posts.body_summary: 550B params (55B active) hybrid Mamba-2 + Transformer MoE; 1M context, 95% on Ruler@1M; ~5× throughput vs open peers; GDPval-AA 'knowledge work' score 1,448. Trained on 10T tokens. Demo: autonomous assistant with Hermes Agent (plan → call tools → delegate → validate → recover).

posts.headline: Read it as an ad for GPUs that happens to be a decent model
posts.body_summary: Claude's verdict on Nemotron: great data-lab, mid-pack model (trails frontier and GLM-5.1 on GDPval-AA). The edge is inference economics on NVIDIA's own silicon (~5× throughput via NVFP4 on Blackwell), not intelligence. The moat is vertical integration + motive — the model is GPU demand-gen. Judge it on inference cost, not capability.

posts.headline: OpenAI ships "Jalapeño," its first in-house inference chip

source.grounding: custom silicon, co-built with Broadcom, for LLM serving

posts.body_summary: First custom silicon, co-developed with Broadcom, purpose-built to serve LLMs (ChatGPT, Codex, the API). @gdb's 'perf-per-watt looking incredible' is a vendor claim — no independent numbers yet; watch for third-party MLPerf.

posts.headline: Clay's production lessons from 350M GTM agents a month

source.grounding: Jeff Barg, on real multi-tenant load

posts.body_summary: 350M GTM agents run per month. −70% LLM cost from caching alone; tool-call caps improve quality (not just cost) by bounding thrash; fairness queues become mandatory once tenants compete for capacity.

posts.headline: The "no-op" test: delete the line — did anything change?

source.grounding: a falsifiable test for skills & prompts

posts.body_summary: Falsifiable craft: remove a line ('be thorough', 'write a detailed commit message'). If output is unchanged, it was a no-op — burning tokens and hurting evaluability. Agent-authored skills are full of them. A test, not a thesis.

posts.headline: Training a legal agent on GLM — the full stack
posts.body_summary: A production post-training case study (not a model release). Three things co-tuned: the eval loop, the agent harness + compaction, and RL post-training of GLM-5.1. Graded on Harvey's Legal Agent Benchmark (LAB). Squarely a domain-build GTM lens.

posts.headline: Inside LAB — what Harvey actually grades
posts.body_summary: 1,250 tasks mirroring real law-firm workflows; 75,000+ expert rubric criteria (atomic, binary); 24 practice areas. All-pass LLM-judged grading — every criterion must pass ('eight of ten risks is materially incomplete'). Sample: review a $458M acquisition data room, 57 criteria across 9 issues. The rubric is the product spec.

posts.headline: How the win was won: a cheap-but-trusted judge, then RL
posts.body_summary: Trusted small-model judge: GPT-5 Mini held >97% alignment to a frontier consensus (GPT-5.5 xhigh + Opus 4.8 Max), and Mini + batched criteria is 40–100× cheaper — what makes high-compute RL affordable. Harness = read+grep over a sandboxed file set, hardened + compaction. Full-parameter RL on GLM-5.1 lifts rubric pass 0.853→0.913 (beats Opus 4.8 Max & GPT-5.5 xhigh); tool calls 16→2. 'The harness made better strategies available; RL taught the model to execute them.'

posts.headline: "Critique of the Agent Model" — where agency actually begins
posts.body_summary: A primary paper giving vocabulary across five axes (goal, identity, decision-making, self-regulation, learning). Its sharp edge: agentic (behavior bolted on by an external harness) vs agentive (capability that lives inside the model itself).

posts.headline: NVFP4 KV cache lands for Blackwell — read the PRs, not the hype
posts.body_summary: vLLM PR #46329 opens native NVFP4 KV cache on SM120/121 via FlashInfer, flag deliberately off until kernels are proven. 3.52× more KV-cache capacity on Gemma-4-E2B (5.1M → 18.1M tokens on one card): same context at a quarter of the memory — longer context and more concurrent users, near-free if accuracy holds.
